title: Guided elastic waves for soft elastomer characterization: an alternative to conventional rheometry
authors: Samuel Croquette, Pierre Chantelot, Daniel A. Kiefer, Claire Prada, Fabrice Lemoult
abstract: Elastic wave propagation is intrinsically sensitive to the mechanical properties of the medium through which it travels. In soft elastomers, this makes guided elastic waves natural probes of viscoelastic and acoustoelastic behavior over a broad frequency range. In this work, we introduce a wave-based mechanical characterization method in which a thin elastomer strip acts as a waveguide supporting multiple in-plane guided modes. By combining stroboscopic measurements of monochromatic wave fields with a theoretical framework that couples frequency-dependent viscoelasticity and elongation-dependent acoustoelasticity, we extract complex-valued dispersion relations for guided modes under controlled static elongation. A dedicated numerical implementation allows these experimental dispersion curves to be quantitatively matched to theory, enabling identification of the material's rheological and hyperelastic parameters. Applied to several commercial silicone elastomers, the method yields mechanical parameters that are consistent with conventional plate-plate rheometry, while extending the accessible frequency range beyond that of conventional techniques. By exploiting the richness of guided-wave dispersion and the sensitivity of waves to both frequency and pre-stress, this approach provides a unified, broadband, and experimentally simple route to the mechanical characterization of soft elastomers.
summary: This paper presents a new method for characterizing soft elastomers using guided elastic waves instead of traditional rheometry. By analyzing wave dispersion under tension, the researchers can extract viscoelastic and hyperelastic properties across a wide frequency range. This approach offers a simpler, broadband alternative for studying the mechanics of soft materials.
main_problem: Developing an alternative to conventional rheometry for characterizing the mechanical, viscoelastic, and acoustoelastic properties of soft elastomers.
main_result: A wave-based characterization method using guided elastic waves was successfully implemented, providing mechanical parameters consistent with plate-plate rheometry but over a much broader frequency range.
method: The method uses stroboscopic measurements of monochromatic wave fields in a thin elastomer strip acting as a waveguide, combined with a numerical implementation to match experimental dispersion curves to a theoretical framework.
model_or_system: The system consists of thin strips of commercial silicone elastomers acting as waveguides for in-plane guided elastic modes under controlled static elongation.
key_observables: Complex-valued dispersion relations for guided modes.
important_parameters: Frequency-dependent viscoelasticity and elongation-dependent acoustoelasticity (hyperelastic parameters).
main_assumptions: The method assumes that the dispersion curves can be quantitatively matched to a theoretical framework coupling viscoelasticity and acoustoelasticity.
paper_structure: The paper introduces a wave-based characterization method, describes the theoretical framework coupling viscoelasticity and acoustoelasticity, details the numerical implementation for matching dispersion curves, and validates the approach using commercial silicone elastomers compared to conventional rheometry.
